metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proving command for the 'no datetime.UTC' negative assertion in AC5, e.g. `grep -rn 'datetime\.UTC[^_]\|datetime\.UTC$' src/rebar tests` exits non-zero (no matches), or add an assertion in test_ruff_ratchet.py that scans source files for the banned alias.
evidence: 'implementation uses datetime.timezone.utc and contains no datetime.UTC reference' — no grep or static-check command is listed for this assertion
'ruff check --select ASYNC,DTZ src/rebar tests' — DTZ rules (DTZ001–DTZ012) flag naive datetime construction, not use of datetime.UTC alias
'The spelling datetime.UTC is prohibited because Ruff target-version remains py310' — the prohibition is stated but not enforced by any listed command
findings.finding: AC5 ('implementation uses datetime.timezone.utc and contains no datetime.UTC reference') has no concrete proving command; the listed verification commands (ruff check --select ASYNC,DTZ, make lint) do not flag datetime.UTC usage because Ruff's DTZ rules target unsafe naive-datetime patterns, not the py3.11+ datetime.UTC alias — the negative assertion goes unmeasured.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 / Testing / Verification section
scenarios: An implementer accidentally writes datetime.UTC (valid syntax in py3.11 but semantically identical to datetime.timezone.utc); all listed checks pass; the criterion is silently violated
CI never fails even though a py310-incompatible alias is present
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to the Testing/Verification section:
```
Negative alias check: grep -rn --include='*.py' 'datetime\.UTC[^_O]' src/rebar tests && echo FOUND && exit 1 || echo OK
```
Or encode the same check as an assertion in tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py so it runs as part of the Configuration pytest tier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the proposed `datetime.datetime.now().astimezone().date()` replacement satisfies DTZ005 (it does not — `now()` without `tz=` triggers DTZ005); replace with a form such as `datetime.datetime.now(tz=datetime.timezone.utc).astimezone().date()` or document the exact compliant pattern in the plan.
evidence: Plan step 3: 'replacing date.today() with datetime.datetime.now().astimezone().date()'
Ruff DTZ005 fires on datetime.datetime.now() without a tz= keyword argument

pyproject.toml:272 comment: 'keeps the gating UP set from pushing 3.11-only idioms (e.g. datetime.UTC)' — confirming the plan is aware of datetime tz constraints but the proposed now() form is itself tz-naive
AC: 'ruff check --select ASYNC,DTZ src/rebar tests exits 0' — would fail with the proposed replacement
findings.finding: The proposed security-pin fix 'datetime.datetime.now().astimezone().date()' calls datetime.now() without a tz= argument, which Ruff DTZ005 flags — so the fix itself introduces a new DTZ violation, contradicting AC 'ruff check --select ASYNC,DTZ src/rebar tests exits 0'.
scenarios: Implementer follows the plan's stated replacement form verbatim; ruff check --select DTZ still fails on the new datetime.now() call in security_pin.py; AC 2 cannot be satisfied.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's prescribed replacement to `datetime.datetime.now(tz=datetime.timezone.utc).astimezone().date()` — passing `tz=datetime.timezone.utc` to `now()` satisfies DTZ005 while `.astimezone()` converts to the host's local timezone so `.date()` still yields a host-local calendar date.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit Approach step (and Scope note if pyproject.toml is touched) describing the assertion that the Ruff security selector `S` is absent from the configuration, so the last AC item has a corresponding described deliverable.
evidence: AC last bullet: 'tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py asserts the exact Ruff security selector S is absent and no Semgrep configuration is introduced by this story'
Approach steps 1–5 contain no step describing the assertion that S is absent or that Semgrep is not introduced
Scope does not mention a pyproject.toml audit for S absence
findings.finding: The last acceptance criterion — 'tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py asserts the exact Ruff security selector S is absent and no Semgrep configuration is introduced by this story' — has no corresponding step in the Approach section and no described work in Scope that would produce or justify this assertion. It is an orphan criterion.
scenarios: Implementer writes test_ruff_ratchet.py following the Approach steps but omits the S-absent assertion, causing the AC to fail with no prior guidance on what work was required.
findings.suggested_fix: In Approach step 1 or a new step, add: 'test_ruff_ratchet.py also asserts that selector S does not appear in the Ruff [tool.ruff.lint] select list and that no semgrep configuration file is introduced by this story.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a Windows-safe mechanism for the process-TZ test (e.g. `unittest.mock.patch` on `datetime.datetime.now` / `datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp` directly, or document that this test is POSIX-only and gate it with `pytest.mark.skipif(sys.platform == 'win32', ...)`), so the AC can be verified on all supported platforms.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Tests change the process TZ and prove the hourly cluster key is invariant.'
Python docs: `time.tzset()` is documented as 'Availability: Unix' — not available on Windows.
On Windows, `os.environ['TZ'] = 'US/Eastern'` followed by `time.tzset()` raises `AttributeError: module 'time' has no attribute 'tzset'`.
The supported platform matrix explicitly includes Windows developer workstations and Windows CI.
findings.finding: The plan requires tests that 'change the process TZ' to prove UTC invariance, but `time.tzset()` — the standard POSIX mechanism for applying a changed TZ environment variable — is not available on Windows. On Windows, `os.environ['TZ']` changes have no effect on Python's timezone resolution, and calling `time.tzset()` raises AttributeError. This means the timezone-invariance tests either crash on Windows or silently become trivial no-ops that don't exercise the property they claim to prove.
findings.location: Approach step 2: 'Tests change the process TZ and prove the hourly cluster key is invariant'; AC: 'tests prove integer epoch timestamps produce the same UTC hour cluster under at least two distinct process TZ settings'
scenarios: Windows developer workstation or Windows CI runner: running `pytest -q tests/unit/test_ruff_ratchet.py` crashes with `AttributeError: module 'time' has no attribute 'tzset'`, blocking the AC verification step entirely.

Alternatively, if the test author guards the tzset call, the TZ change silently has no effect on Windows, making the test pass trivially without actually proving timezone invariance — a false green on a key correctness property.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the TZ-environment approach with `unittest.mock.patch('datetime.datetime', ...)` or `freezegun` to supply controlled datetime values in the test, avoiding any platform-specific timezone-setting mechanism. Alternatively, mark the TZ-manipulation tests with `@pytest.mark.skipif(sys.platform == 'win32', reason='time.tzset not available on Windows')` and add a complementary mock-based test that covers Windows.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement in the Approach (or a dedicated compatibility note) that audits consumers of validate_checks.py for dependence on naive/local datetimes, and describe the rollback path (e.g., revert commit or feature flag) if the UTC-aware datetime breaks downstream behavior.
evidence: Approach step 2: 'Integer epoch seconds are converted with datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(created, tz=datetime.timezone.utc)'
The prior call (datetime.fromtimestamp without tz) returns a local-time naive datetime; the replacement returns a UTC-aware datetime — a different object type and value in non-UTC environments
No rollback plan, no expand-contract sequencing, and no statement that callers are audited for dependence on the previous behavior
findings.finding: The plan changes the runtime behavior of datetime.fromtimestamp() in validate_checks.py from returning a local-timezone-aware or naive datetime to returning an explicit UTC datetime, which is an observable behavioral change for any caller that consumed those datetime objects. No consumer-impact analysis, expand-contract sequence, or rollback path is described.
scenarios: A caller downstream of validate_checks.py that compares or serializes the returned datetime without timezone awareness will silently change behavior in non-UTC CI/CD environments after this change lands, with no rollback available.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AGENTS.md entry or ADR capturing the two new datetime-semantic contracts: (a) validation timestamps are stored/interpreted as UTC, and (b) security-pin cadence uses host-local calendar date — and explain why each was chosen.
evidence: 'Define validation timestamp semantics as UTC.'
'Preserve security-rule cadence as a host-local calendar-date decision by replacing date.today() with datetime.datetime.now().astimezone().date().'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
The plan notes '125d-ee6f-7af0-4200 depends on this story and extends that file later', indicating downstream consumers of this contract exist.
findings.finding: The plan introduces two new semantic contracts — 'validation timestamps are UTC' and 'security-rule cadence uses host-local calendar date' — that cross component boundaries and will affect future contributors, but no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update is included to capture these decisions.
scenarios: A future developer adding a new timestamp field chooses UTC or local time inconsistently because the project-level convention is only implicit in scattered test assertions.
A future developer working on security-pin logic doesn't realize the local-calendar-date choice was deliberate (vs. UTC) and reverts or changes it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief AGENTS.md section (e.g. '## Datetime conventions') stating: 'Event/validation timestamps use UTC (datetime.timezone.utc). Security-rule cadence checks use host-local calendar date (datetime.now().astimezone().date()) to match the operator's expectation of "today".' Reference the owning test file for each.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale sentence in the Approach explaining WHY fixing the three sites (rather than suppressing) is chosen: e.g. that it delivers real semantic correctness, matches the established codebase pattern (`tz=timezone.utc` in `review_bot/reconcile.py` and `adf.py`), and makes cluster-key behaviour provably TZ-invariant rather than accidentally correct.
evidence: Plan quote: 'Rejected: blanket DTZ ignores.' — only a rejection, no affirmative justification.

The rest of the codebase uses the timezone-aware pattern: src/rebar/review_bot/reconcile.py:112 `datetime.fromtimestamp(epoch_seconds, tz=timezone.utc)`, src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adf.py:193 `datetime.fromtimestamp(epoch_ms / 1000, tz=timezone.utc)`.
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'we fix the sites because...' appears in the Approach or Context sections.
findings.finding: The plan states 'Rejected: blanket DTZ ignores' but provides no positive rationale for WHY fixing the three sites (rather than per-site suppression) is the chosen approach — e.g. that it delivers real semantic correctness (tz-aware datetimes prevent host-TZ-sensitive bugs), aligns with the codebase's established pattern (review_bot/reconcile.py and adf.py already use tz=timezone.utc), and makes the cluster-key behavior testable and invariant rather than accidentally correct.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Rejected: blanket DTZ ignores'; no corresponding positive rationale sentence present
scenarios: An implementer who encounters the plan without the broader codebase context may not understand the semantic importance of the fix versus a suppression, making it harder to review or extend the work correctly.
The downstream story (125d-ee6f-7af0-4200) that extends test_ruff_ratchet.py would benefit from understanding the positive rationale for why the fix approach was chosen.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Fixing the sites rather than suppressing is preferred because it delivers real semantic correctness (tz-aware datetimes are immune to host-TZ changes), aligns with the existing codebase pattern (review_bot/reconcile.py and adf.py already use tz=timezone.utc for fromtimestamp), and makes the UTC-hour cluster key behaviour provably invariant rather than accidentally correct on UTC-configured hosts.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
